title: The Big Picture — A History of Python Packaging from 2008 to Today

transcript.text: Welcome to a slightly different video. Instead of a how-to, today I want to tell the story of how Python packaging arrived at its current state. We'll go from 2008 to 2024 and look at why so many tools exist and why people have such strong opinions.

Chapter one — the dark ages, 2008 to 2013. Pip was new, virtualenv was a third-party hack, and most people just installed everything globally and prayed. distutils existed, setuptools existed but was contentious. Eggs were a real thing. Wheels did not exist yet.

Chapter two — the standardization era, 2013 to 2018. PEP 427 introduced the wheel format in 2013. PEP 405 had already added venv to the standard library back in Python 3.3. By the time of Python 3.5, the basic shape of pip plus venv plus wheel was set.

Chapter three — the Conda parallel universe. Around 2014, Anaconda introduced Conda, which solved a different problem — non-Python scientific dependencies and BLAS routing. The Python community has had two ecosystems running in parallel ever since: PyPI plus pip for general purposes, and Conda plus conda-forge for scientific computing.

Chapter four — the project-management era, 2018 to 2022. People realized pip alone was not enough. Pipenv launched in 2017 with much fanfare and then stagnated. Poetry launched in 2018 and slowly became the de facto standard for serious projects. PEP 517 and PEP 518 standardized pyproject.toml as the build configuration file.

Chapter five — the version-management era. pyenv had existed since 2011 as a port of Ruby's rbenv, but it became central to many people's workflow around 2019 to 2021 when it became normal to need different Python versions per project. The combo pyenv plus Poetry, or pyenv plus pip plus venv, defined what a 'modern' Python project looked like for a few years.

Chapter six — the Rust-rewrite wave, 2023 to 2024. ruff replaced flake8 plus isort plus black for linting. Then Astral released uv, doing for packaging what ruff did for linting — same broad function, dramatically faster, statically linked, written in Rust. Pixi from Prefix Dev does similar work in the Conda ecosystem.

Where does that leave a developer in 2024? Honestly, you have a lot of options. Plain venv works for hello-world. Poetry is mature and has a plugin ecosystem. uv is the new, fast, integrated choice. Conda still owns scientific computing. Pixi is interesting if you want a Conda replacement that feels modern.

I want to be neutral here. This video is not telling you which tool to pick. It's telling you the lineage so when someone says 'just use venv' or 'just use Poetry' or 'just use uv,' you understand what era of advice they are coming from.

A few patterns repeat. First: every generation of tooling promises 'the unified one tool to rule them all,' and the next generation arrives a few years later. Second: macOS-specific issues come up roughly every two years tied to OS upgrades. Third: scientific computing always has its own answer.

Some history I find genuinely useful for context. distutils was deprecated in Python 3.10 and removed in Python 3.12. setuptools is still alive but is no longer the only build backend — Hatchling, PDM-backend, Flit, and Poetry-core are all valid. pyproject.toml is the only config file you should care about for new projects.

If you are a beginner watching this: don't let the tooling overwhelm you. Pick whatever your team uses. The deltas between modern options are speed and ergonomics, not capability. All of pip plus venv, Poetry, and uv can ship a working Python application in 2024.

Some people watching will want a recommendation anyway, even though I said this video is neutral. My personal preference for new projects in 2024 is uv. My justification is the same as everyone else's: speed and integration. But that's a preference statement, not the message of this video.

If you stuck around for the history: thanks. The episode notes have links to the original PEPs, the Anaconda founding story, the Astral blog posts on uv's design, and a timeline diagram you can use in your own presentations.

Coming up in future videos: a deep dive on PEP 723 inline script metadata, a comparison of build backends for library authors, and a look at how Python's Steering Council coordinates packaging standards. Subscribe if any of that interests you.

Final thought. Tooling is a means, not an end. The reason we have venv, pyenv, Poetry, and uv is that Python made some early decisions about packaging that left gaps, and the community has been filling those gaps with increasingly clever tools. The arc bends toward more integration and less ceremony, and that's a good thing.

Take everything you saw in this video as context for the more opinionated tutorials out there. When someone says 'X is the only way,' check the publication date. When someone says 'Y replaces Z,' look at what Z was solving and decide whether you have that problem.
Thanks for watching this longer-form one. Like and subscribe for more retrospective Python content. See you in the next video!

key_points: Tangential history of Python packaging from 2008 to 2024
Maps tools to eras: pip+venv (standard), Poetry (project mgmt), pyenv (versions), uv/ruff (Rust wave 2023-2024)
Stays NEUTRAL on which tool to pick — context, not recommendation
Notes macOS-specific issues recur every ~2 years
Author's offhand preference: uv for new projects, but framed as preference not the video's thesis
